1.blurb: The Sengoku Jidai of warring states, which Sekigahara ended in 1600, was the long aftermath of the Genpei War and the rise of the samurai class — the closing chapters of the Ancient Japan TL pick up the warrior aristocracy from its emergence in the late Heian period through the Genpei War (1180–85) and the founding of the first shogunate. Edo Japan inherits a martial aristocracy with five centuries of pre-history.

The Korean Peninsula that Hideyoshi devastated in his 1592–98 invasions was the country whose long earlier history is covered in the Ancient Korea TL — Korean kingdoms, Buddhist culture, and the Three Kingdoms period out of which the Joseon Dynasty (the Korean polity Hideyoshi attacked) eventually emerged. The Imjin War left scars on the Korean Peninsula that would shape Korean attitudes toward Japan for centuries.

Shah Abbas the Great of Safavid Persia (reigned 1588–1629) was almost exactly contemporary with Ieyasu and the early Tokugawa shoguns. Both rulers transformed minor regional dynasties into world-historical empires; both built capital cities that became wonders of their respective worlds (Isfahan and Edo); both faced the structural problem of how to convert a tribal-warrior aristocracy into a centralized administrative state. The two solutions — Abbas's ghulam revolution and Ieyasu's bakuhan system — were structurally similar even though geographically isolated from each other.

While Ieyasu was consolidating Tokugawa rule in the early 1600s, the Ottoman Empire was at the height of its early-modern power under Sultan Ahmed I and his successors, having recently completed the great century of Selim and Süleyman. The Tokugawa and the Ottomans were nearly opposite poles of early-modern Eurasian governance: the Tokugawa closed their country and ruled through hereditary aristocratic federation, while the Ottomans projected military force across three continents through a slave-soldier army.

2.blurb: The closest contemporary parallel to the Tokugawa daimyo system was actually the Ottoman timar and beylerbey provincial governance system, which similarly balanced central authority against locally-rooted military aristocracies. Both systems lasted roughly two and a half centuries before being replaced by more centralized modern states (the Tokugawa fell in 1868, the Ottoman provincial system was reformed under the Tanzimat in the mid-nineteenth century). The structural similarity is striking despite the absence of any direct contact.

The Habsburg-Tokugawa structural parallel deserves a moment. Both presided over loose federations of semi-autonomous noble territories under a central monarchy. Both balanced rival aristocratic factions against each other. Both controlled commerce through chartered monopolies. Both restricted religious heterodoxy as a core state interest. The Habsburgs spent the seventeenth century fighting the Ottoman Empire across the Balkans while the Tokugawa shut Japan against the same Iberian Catholic powers the Habsburgs were defending; the two systems were responding to the same wave of religious-political pressure from opposite ends of Eurasia.

The Buddhism that Tokugawa Japan made into a registered state religion through the terauke system had arrived in Japan in the sixth century CE and developed into the major sectarian forms (Tendai, Shingon, Pure Land, Zen, Nichiren) over the Heian and Kamakura periods covered in the Ancient Japan TL. The terauke system was a state appropriation of an institution that had been Japanese for over a thousand years by the time the Tokugawa formalized it as the population-registration mechanism.

The sun goddess Amaterasu, central deity of the Ise Grand Shrine that Tokugawa Japan continued to rebuild every twenty years, was the divine ancestor of the imperial line according to the eighth-century Kojiki and Nihon Shoki — the foundational mythologies covered in the Ancient Japan TL. The Tokugawa shogunate's careful ceremonial deference to the imperial court at Kyoto, even while holding all real power, depended on the Amaterasu-descent claim that Edo-period Shinto and Kokugaku scholars would intermittently emphasize and that the Meiji Restoration would mobilize against the shogunate.

3.blurb: The Tokugawa anti-Christian persecution and the Spanish Inquisition's persecution of crypto-Jews and crypto-Muslims (the same period, comparable methodology, opposite religious direction) are the two clearest cases of state-sponsored religious purge in early-modern history. The Safavid forced conversion of Iran from Sunni to Shia Islam was a third — same period, similar levels of state coercion, similar use of public ritual humiliation tests, similar success in changing a country's religious composition over multiple generations. The early-modern state had figured out how to break populations to a religious orthodoxy, and Japan was one of the cases.

The Joseon Korean kingdom that maintained relations with Tokugawa Japan through the Tsushima channel was the successor to the Goryeo Dynasty that ended in 1392 — the closing pages of the Ancient Korea TL trace Korean political evolution into the early Joseon period. The diplomatic missions Joseon sent to Tokugawa Japan twelve times between 1607 and 1811 were a continuation of Korean–Japanese state-to-state relations that had operated, with interruptions, for over a thousand years.

4.blurb: The Mughal Emperor Aurangzeb (reigned 1658–1707) was the contemporary of Tsunayoshi and the Genroku era. Both rulers presided over imperial peaks (Mughal India reached its largest territorial extent under Aurangzeb; Tokugawa Japan reached its greatest cultural flowering during Genroku) that obscured underlying structural decay. Both states would enter terminal decline within two generations of these peaks — the Mughal in the eighteenth century, the Tokugawa in the early nineteenth — for similar reasons of fiscal exhaustion, military stagnation, and the inability to respond to changing geopolitical conditions.

The Qing Dynasty under the Kangxi Emperor (reigned 1661–1722) was at its early peak during the Genroku era, building the imperial framework that would make High Qing China one of the most populous and prosperous states in pre-industrial world history. The Qing and the Tokugawa were the two great East Asian early-modern states; both maintained restrictive trade policies, both faced European pressure in the nineteenth century, and both ultimately collapsed within a century of each other. The difference is that the Tokugawa transition produced the rapid Meiji modernization while the Qing transition produced a much messier century of revolution and civil war.

The Dutch Republic that maintained the Dejima trading post at Nagasaki was the same commercial power that, in the same period, was supplanting the Ottoman Empire as the dominant naval and commercial force in the eastern Mediterranean. The Dutch global commercial empire of the seventeenth century — the same one that had reached Manhattan, Cape Town, and Batavia — included Dejima as one of its more peculiar outposts: a constrained trading station maintained at considerable expense for the steady supply of Japanese silver and copper that fed the Dutch arbitrage networks across the Indian Ocean.

5.blurb: Cervantes published Don Quixote in 1605, the same year the Ottoman Empire was at the height of its early-modern power under Ahmed I and the Tokugawa shogunate was being founded. The novel's central insight — that a hereditary warrior aristocracy without wars to fight loses its function and becomes either ridiculous or pathological — applied as much to the late-Ottoman Janissary corps and the Tokugawa samurai class as to the Castilian hidalgos Cervantes was satirizing. The Akō rōnin's response to the same problem was to perform the warrior function literally, with a vendetta that made them into national heroes; the Ottoman Janissary response (covered in the Ottoman Empire TL) was to become a political liability that Mahmud II eventually massacred in 1826.

The Neo-Confucian framework that Tokugawa-era thinkers used to interpret the Akō incident — debating whether the rōnin had honored or violated the proper hierarchy of obligations — had been imported from China through Korea over the preceding centuries. The Joseon Korean state was, in the same period, a much more thoroughgoing Neo-Confucian society than Tokugawa Japan, with civil service examinations, scholar-official governance, and Confucian ritual integrated into nearly every aspect of public life. The Tokugawa adoption of Neo-Confucianism was a much more selective and instrumental project than the parallel Korean version.

7.blurb: The structural decline of late-Edo Japan paralleled almost exactly the structural decline of the Ottoman Empire across the long eighteenth and nineteenth centuries — both states were once-formidable empires whose internal arrangements had become incapable of responding to European industrial-military pressure. The Ottoman cycle of failed reform programs (the Tulip Period, Selim III's New Order, the Tanzimat) is the structural twin of the Tokugawa Kyōhō-Kansei-Tenpō reform sequence. Both ended in collapse, the Tokugawa first in 1868 and the Ottomans more slowly through 1918.

The Qing Dynasty in China was undergoing structurally similar pressures across the same period — peasant uprisings (the White Lotus Rebellion of 1796–1804 and eventually the catastrophic Taiping Rebellion of 1850–64), fiscal stress, the foreign-pressure problem in the same Western-power form. The Qing collapse came later (1911) and the consequences were much more violent than the Meiji Restoration. The structural comparison is informative: pre-industrial East Asian states confronting industrial European pressure tended to decay along similar lines, even when the specific institutional details were quite different.

8.blurb: Joseon Korea, which had maintained the Hermit Kingdom posture even more strictly than Tokugawa Japan, would face its own forced opening in 1876 (the Treaty of Ganghwa, imposed by Meiji Japan on Joseon Korea using exactly the gunboat diplomacy that Perry had used on Japan). The annexation of Korea in 1910 by the Meiji-era Japanese Empire is the long shadow of the events at Uraga in 1853 — the country forced open became the country forcing others open, and the Korean peninsula bore the consequences for the next thirty-five years.
